name: Jnanesvar
dates_notes: Thirteenth-century Maharashtra author; the traditional biographical range is c. 1275-1296, with the *Jñāneśvarī* dated to 1290 in the tradition.
core_thesis: Jñāneśvar is the Marathi philosopher-poet whose *Jñāneśvarī* gives a philosophical vernacular commentary on the *Bhagavadgītā* and whose *Amṛtānubhava* is an independent metaphysical work. His Vedānta is Advaita-oriented but presented through Marathi, Nāth, and devotional idioms rather than the Sanskrit scholastic format. The safe claim is literary and doctrinally broad: he expounds non-dual realization, the relation of knowledge and devotion, and the experience of the self through a regional philosophical language. The local sources are archive OCRs, so exact verse-level claims remain deferred.

engaged_works.title: Jnanesvari
engaged_works.ascription_notes: The Marathi *Jñāneśvarī* is securely attributed to Jñāneśvar in the Maharashtrian tradition and modern scholarship.
engaged_works.summary: A Marathi commentary on the *Bhagavadgītā*, also called *Bhāvārtha-Dīpikā*. It translates and interprets the Gītā into a regional philosophical idiom, joining Advaita-oriented non-dual realization with devotion and yogic discipline. Its importance for this corpus is that it is a major vernacular Vedānta work rather than a Sanskrit scholastic bhāṣya. The local text is Internet Archive OCR of a Marathi/Devanāgarī scan.

engaged_works.title: Amrtanubhava
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely attributed to Jñāneśvar in the Marathi Vedānta and Vārkarī traditions.
engaged_works.summary: An independent Marathi metaphysical work associated with Jñāneśvar. It treats realization and the relation of word, knowledge, and direct experience in a non-dual register, without being simply a commentary on another scripture. The local corpus contains Internet Archive OCR of an English rendering of the Marathi text; exact passage claims are not asserted here.
